synthesis.summary: IBM surged 6.1% on 3x normal volume after announcing a $10B quantum computing investment with a 2029 commercialization target, signaling a major strategic commitment that reshaped near-term growth expectations. The scale of the capital allocation and the concrete timeline drove outsized buying interest. The RSI at 84 and 5-day gain of nearly 25% suggest the move may be somewhat extended relative to the news catalyst alone.

Barry Diller's IAC/InterActiveCorp (People) made a move to take MGM Resorts private, which directly triggered the 17.7% surge on 4x normal volume. Privatization bids typically price in a significant premium to the pre-announcement market price, which explains both the magnitude of the move and the overbought RSI reading.

ARM jumped 15.6% after Nvidia unveiled a new PC chip built on ARM architecture, with investors treating the launch as a demand signal for ARM's instruction set licensing even though ARM was barely referenced in the announcement. The move looks outsized relative to ARM's actual direct involvement, suggesting speculative momentum is amplifying the reaction beyond what the product news alone justifies.

AAOI surged ~20% today with no high-impact news to explain the move — the only available article is a retrospective return calculator piece, which carries no new fundamental information. The magnitude of the move, combined with below-average relative volume (0.63), suggests options flow, short squeeze dynamics, or sector rotation rather than a news catalyst.

ASAN is up 18% today with volume only modestly above average, but no news in the supplied set explains the move. The single news item is a generic "movers" roundup with no detail on what drove the gain. The 5-day move of 37% suggests a multi-day momentum run that may be driven by options flow, short covering, or sector rotation — none of which are documented here.

MDB surged 20.6% on nearly double its average volume, but the only supplied news is a macro piece about oil prices and geopolitical tensions — nothing that explains a move of this magnitude for MongoDB specifically. This is almost certainly driven by a company-specific catalyst (likely an earnings beat or guidance raise) not captured in the provided news items.

NBIS is up ~16% today on elevated RSI and flagged as overbought, but the supplied news items are low-impact and neither reveals a specific catalyst — the Benzinga article asking "why is Nebius surging" suggests the move's cause was not clearly established even in the press. With relative volume at only 0.65x, this doesn't appear to be a broad institutional push, pointing more toward options flow, short covering, or a catalyst not captured in the provided news.

RDW is down nearly 16% on heavy selling pressure, but none of the supplied news items carry high impact or identify a specific catalyst — no earnings miss, contract loss, guidance cut, or material corporate event is cited. The Benzinga articles acknowledge the move but don't explain its cause, and relative volume (0.72) is actually below average, suggesting this isn't a broad institutional flush. The move remains unexplained by available news.

No fundamental catalyst is identifiable in the supplied news — the medium-impact item describes the squeeze in progress rather than explaining what started it. SPCE is up ~130% over five days with volume running 9x normal and RSI near 90, a pattern consistent with a short squeeze feeding on itself: short sellers covering into a thinly traded, heavily shorted name amplifies price action without any underlying business news driving it.

Snowflake surged 11% following what the company described as its best quarterly results in years, driven by a standout performance from a specific product. The magnitude and direction of the move align directly with a strong earnings beat, consistent with the elevated volume (1.49x) and RSI hitting overbought territory at 89.8.

Nvidia's unveiling of a new AI PC chip directly threatens Intel's core PC processor business, with analysts flagging structural competitive risk for Intel in the segment. The product launch shifts market share expectations toward Nvidia (and to a lesser extent Arm), explaining the ~6% drop on elevated volume.

NOK surged 10.24% on significantly above-average volume, but the only supplied news — a generic "whale activity" roundup covering 10 IT stocks — does not explain a move of this magnitude or provide any NOK-specific catalyst. The move is likely driven by options flow, short covering, or sector rotation that isn't captured in the available news.

ONDS is up 3.1% today and 50% over the past five days, with RSI at 70 signaling overbought conditions, but the only available news is a low-impact Benzinga article that offers no identifiable catalyst. The multi-day surge likely reflects momentum continuation or options/retail flow from an earlier catalyst not captured in the supplied news.

ORCL is up 10.66% on the day with volume running 74% above average, but none of the supplied news items explain the move — the only ORCL-specific item is a low-impact "what's going on" curiosity piece with no disclosed catalyst, and the macro headline is unrelated. A move of this magnitude (30% over 5 days, RSI at 81) likely reflects a major earnings beat, guidance raise, or large contract announcement that is not present in the provided news feed.

RKLB is down 13.3% with no company-specific catalyst visible in the supplied news — the Benzinga "what's happening" piece (id 8c700e3f7ddfbf33) acknowledges the drop but provides no underlying reason. A peer (Firefly Aerospace) is also falling today, suggesting possible sector rotation or shared sentiment pressure, but the specific driver for a move of this magnitude is not explained by any available news item.

DELL is up ~10% today and 57% over the past five days, hitting an all-time high, but none of the supplied news items carry a high-impact catalyst — no earnings release, guidance update, or major contract announcement is present in the feed. The medium-impact items reference Nvidia's AI PC chip unveiling (which could peripherally benefit Dell as a hardware partner) and an HPE earnings preview that frames Dell as the AI infrastructure benchmark, but neither directly triggers a move of this magnitude. The surge is most likely driven by options flow, momentum chasing into overbought conditions (RSI 91), or a catalyst not captured in this news set.
